stem: A 30-year-old G2P2 woman, three weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding her well term infant, presents to her GP with a 24-hour history of a tender, hot, wedge-shaped erythematous area in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. She feels feverish and shivery, has been continuing frequent feeds and effective expression after feeds, and the area has continued to enlarge over the last 12 hours. There is no fluctuance, no skin breakdown and no axillary adenopathy. She is otherwise well. Her recorded drug allergy is to phenoxymethylpenicillin during childhood, when she developed an urticarial rash and lip swelling within 1 hour of a dose; she has never tolerated a penicillin or cephalosporin since. She has no other allergies, has not had antibiotics in the past 12 months, has no recent hospital contact and is not from a community with known high MRSA prevalence. She wishes to continue breastfeeding throughout treatment and her infant has no medical conditions and was born at term with no neonatal complications.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 degC, HR 104 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Wedge-shaped erythema upper outer left breast, hot and tender, no fluctuance, no skin breakdown, no axillary lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis clear. Bedside ultrasound not yet performed.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate oral antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Flucloxacillin 500 mg orally four times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin would be first-line but is contraindicated given an immediate hypersensitivity reaction (urticaria, angioedema within 1 hour) to phenoxymethylpenicillin (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Cefalexin 500 mg orally four times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Cephalosporins are avoided when the penicillin reaction was an immediate (Type 1) hypersensitivity with angioedema, because of unpredictable cross-reactivity in true IgE-mediated allergy (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Clindamycin 450 mg orally three times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Clindamycin is the eTG-recommended alternative for lactational mastitis in patients with immediate penicillin hypersensitivity; it covers S. aureus including most CA-MRSA, is compatible with breastfeeding per LactMed and concentrates well in breast tissue (Therapeutic Guidelines; LactMed).

options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate contains a penicillin and is contraindicated in immediate penicillin hypersensitivity; it would also be unnecessarily broad (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: TMP-SMX is reserved for confirmed MRSA cover and is avoided in mothers of infants younger than 1 month due to a theoretical kernicterus risk; clindamycin is preferred at three weeks postpartum if penicillins are not an option (Therapeutic Guidelines; LactMed).

explanation.summary: Lactational mastitis with systemic features warrants oral antibiotics for 5-7 days plus effective milk removal. First-line is flucloxacillin; with immediate penicillin hypersensitivity the eTG alternative is clindamycin, which is breastfeeding-compatible (LactMed).
key_points: Most common pathogen: Staphylococcus aureus
Continue breastfeeding/expressing - drainage is therapeutic
Immediate (Type 1) penicillin allergy excludes cephalosporins as well
Clindamycin covers most CA-MRSA and concentrates in breast tissue
TMP-SMX avoided in mothers feeding infants <1 month (kernicterus risk)
explanation.pearls: If the mother fails to improve at 48 hours of appropriate antibiotics, image with ultrasound for abscess before broadening cover.

subtopic_detail: Lactational mastitis - antibiotic choice in penicillin allergy

stem: A 27-year-old primiparous woman, 6 weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding her thriving term infant on demand, attends a same-day GP appointment for a 14-hour history of a discrete, tender lump in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast noticed after a long night feed. The overlying skin shows faint pink mottling but no defined erythema, no hot spot and no skin breakdown. She has no rigors, no flu-like myalgia and no nipple cracks. The infant has been feeding effectively from both breasts and weight gain is appropriate; the mother reports no recent change in feeding pattern other than the baby sleeping a longer 5-hour stretch overnight for the past two nights. There is no fluctuance and no axillary lymphadenopathy. She has had no antibiotics, is on no medications and is otherwise well.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 degC, HR 78 bpm, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 16.
data_table.Examination: Tender 3 cm lump lower inner right breast, faint overlying pink mottling, no defined erythema, no fluctuance, no axillary adenopathy.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Commence oral flucloxacillin 500 mg orally four times daily for 7 days now
options.rationale: There are no systemic features and no defined erythema; empirical antibiotics are not indicated for a blocked duct without bacterial mastitis features (ABA; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Continue feeding from both breasts, apply cold packs between feeds and review in 24 hours
options.rationale: This is a blocked duct (focal lump, no fever, no systemic upset). Current ABM and ABA guidance emphasises gentle lymphatic drainage, cold (not aggressive deep massage or heat) and ongoing physiological feeding; review at 24 hours to detect progression to inflammatory or bacterial mastitis (ABA).

options.text: Aggressive deep breast massage every 2 hours together with hot compresses for 48 hours
options.rationale: Updated ABM and ABA guidance specifically advises against deep massage and heat, which worsen tissue oedema and may promote progression to inflammatory mastitis (ABM 2022).

options.text: Cease feeding from the affected breast and pump-and-dump for at least 48 hours
options.rationale: Cessation of feeding worsens milk stasis and risks progression to abscess; breastfeeding milk is safe for the infant (ABA).

options.text: Urgent breast ultrasound and surgical referral for incision and drainage of the lump
options.rationale: There is no fluctuance, no defined erythema and no systemic features to suggest abscess; imaging is reserved for non-resolving mastitis or fluctuant collections (ABM).

explanation.summary: The lactation inflammation spectrum runs from ductal narrowing/blocked duct (focal lump, no systemic features) through inflammatory mastitis (erythema, pain) to bacterial mastitis (systemic features) to abscess. Current evidence-based care de-emphasises deep massage and heat; the cornerstones are physiological feeding, lymphatic drainage and cold for symptom control.
key_points: Blocked duct: focal lump, no fever, no defined erythema
Inflammatory mastitis: erythema and pain, no systemic features
Bacterial mastitis: above plus fever/tachycardia, or non-resolution
Abscess: fluctuant collection on USS
Cold preferred over heat per ABM 2022 protocol
explanation.pearls: A long overnight feed gap is the classic precipitant of a blocked duct in an established 6-week breastfeeding dyad.

subtopic_detail: Blocked duct vs inflammatory mastitis vs bacterial mastitis discrimination

stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 woman, 4 weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding, was diagnosed with right-breast bacterial mastitis 5 days ago and commenced flucloxacillin 500 mg orally four times daily with continued feeding and expression. Her fevers settled within 48 hours but the erythematous area has now developed a discrete, fluctuant tender lump over the past 24 hours despite ongoing antibiotics and effective drainage. She is afebrile today, haemodynamically stable, and the skin overlying the lump is intact, non-necrotic and not under tension. Bedside ultrasound performed by the GP demonstrates a well-defined, unilocular hypoechoic collection measuring 38 mm x 24 mm at 1.5 cm depth, with no overlying skin necrosis. She has no recurrent abscess history, no immunosuppression, no diabetes and wishes to continue breastfeeding. She is comfortable being managed in primary care if the lesion is amenable to office-based drainage and the local Breast Centre offers ultrasound-guided procedures on a same-day basis.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 degC, HR 84 bpm, BP 122/76 mmHg.
data_table.Examination: Right breast, 4 cm fluctuant tender mass, overlying skin intact and non-necrotic, no axillary adenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside USS: unilocular 38 x 24 mm hypoechoic collection, 1.5 cm depth, no skin necrosis. Hb 124 g/L, WCC 11.2 x10^9/L, CRP 48 mg/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue current oral flucloxacillin and review in 5 days without drainage
options.rationale: A drained collection requires drainage to resolve; antibiotics alone are inadequate and risk chronicity, fistula and lactiferous duct injury (ABM Clinical Protocol 36).

options.text: Refer for ultrasound-guided needle aspiration with culture; continue antibiotics, reaspirate if needed
options.rationale: For a lactational abscess under approximately 5 cm with intact non-necrotic skin, ultrasound-guided needle aspiration (often serial) with cultured aspirate and continued antibiotics is first-line; it preserves duct architecture, allows ongoing breastfeeding and avoids a surgical wound that complicates feeding (ABM; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Refer for theatre incision and drainage with packing under general anaesthetic
options.rationale: Surgical I&D is reserved for very large abscesses, multiloculated collections, or where the skin is thinned/necrotic; it leaves a wound that may interfere with feeding and is unnecessary for a 4 cm unilocular collection (ABM).

options.text: Stop breastfeeding from the affected breast and continue antibiotics only
options.rationale: Cessation worsens milk stasis and slows resolution; current guidance is to continue feeding from both breasts where the infant will latch, or to express if the infant refuses (ABA).

options.text: Switch to oral clindamycin 450 mg three times daily and observe without drainage
options.rationale: Antibiotic broadening without drainage is inadequate; an established collection needs source control (ABM).

explanation.summary: Lactational breast abscesses require source control. Ultrasound-guided needle aspiration with cultured aspirate is first-line for abscesses up to around 5 cm with intact skin and is repeated as needed; surgical drainage is reserved for larger, multiloculated, or skin-compromised collections.
key_points: Continue breastfeeding throughout treatment
USS-guided aspiration preserves duct anatomy
Send aspirate for culture to guide ongoing antibiotics
Surgical I&D for large, multiloculated, or skin-necrosed abscesses
Antibiotics alone are inadequate once a collection has formed
explanation.pearls: An apparently improving mastitis that develops a new fluctuant area at day 5 is an abscess until ultrasound proves otherwise.

subtopic_detail: Lactational breast abscess - drainage strategy

stem: A 28-year-old primiparous woman attends with her 3-week-old term infant for a same-day appointment because she is convinced her milk supply has dropped. The baby is now feeding for 60-80 minutes per feed, 10-12 times in 24 hours, and is unsettled between feeds. She produces only 30 mL when she expresses after a feed and feels her breasts are softer than they were at 10 days. The infant had a birthweight of 3.4 kg, was discharged at 3.15 kg on day 4 (regained birthweight by day 12), and today weighs 3.55 kg on the practice scales. The infant is alert, has 6-8 heavy wet nappies and 3-4 mustard-yellow stools per day, has no jaundice, and feeds with audible swallowing during feeds. She has been advised by family to start formula top-ups. She is not on any medications, has no breast surgery history, has normal-appearing breasts that grew through pregnancy, and breastfed her older nieces' children briefly without difficulty.

data_table.Infant assessment: Birthweight 3.4 kg; day 4 discharge 3.15 kg; today 3.55 kg (mean gain ~30 g/day from nadir).
data_table.Feeding: 10-12 feeds/24h, audible swallowing, 6-8 heavy wet nappies, 3-4 mustard stools/day.
data_table.Maternal: Soft breasts, no surgery, infant alert.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence formula top-ups of 60 mL after each breastfeed to meet caloric requirements
options.rationale: Supplementation without an indication for it suppresses prolactin-driven supply and undermines breastfeeding; growth and output are normal (ABA; RANZCOG).

options.text: Reassure: growth, nappy output and feeding behaviour indicate adequate transfer; review in 1 week
options.rationale: Soft breasts after the early hyperlactation phase, low expressed volumes (a poor proxy for transfer), and cluster-feeding behaviour are normal at 3 weeks. Adequate weight gain (>20-30 g/day after nadir), regaining birthweight by 2 weeks, and 6+ heavy wet nappies confirm adequate intake; perceived low supply without objective markers is the most common cause of unnecessary supplementation (ABA; RCH; ABM).

options.text: Commence domperidone 10 mg three times daily as a pharmacological galactagogue
options.rationale: Galactagogues are considered only after a structured assessment has confirmed inadequate supply, identified a reversible cause, and addressed feeding technique; supply here is objectively adequate (ABM Clinical Protocol 9).

options.text: Arrange measured pre-feed and post-feed test-weighs over 24 hours at home
options.rationale: Test-weighing is anxiety-provoking, prone to error and rarely changes management when growth and output are reassuring; it is not first-line (ABA; ABM).

options.text: Refer for maternal pituitary MRI to exclude Sheehan syndrome as the cause
options.rationale: There is no clinical suspicion for Sheehan (no severe PPH, no other pituitary dysfunction); imaging is not indicated (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Perceived low supply is far more common than true low supply. Assess transfer using objective markers (weight gain >20-30 g/day after nadir, regaining birthweight by 2 weeks, 6+ heavy wet nappies, mustard stools, audible swallowing) before introducing supplementation or galactagogues.
key_points: Expressed volume is a poor proxy for milk transfer
Cluster feeding and softer breasts are normal after 2-3 weeks
Confirm adequate output with weight + nappies + stools before intervening
Premature top-ups suppress supply (negative feedback)
Galactagogues are second-line after a structured assessment
explanation.pearls: Counsel that breasts feeling 'less full' often reflects normal calibration of supply to demand, not failing supply.

subtopic_detail: Low milk supply - first step in assessment

stem: A 31-year-old primiparous woman is reviewed at home by the midwifery domiciliary service on postnatal day 4 after an uncomplicated vaginal birth of a healthy term infant. She reports both breasts are very full, tight, painful, with diffuse symmetrical firmness and shiny skin. The infant has had a slightly difficult latch this morning because the nipple is flattened by the surrounding oedema. She feels mildly flushed and warm but checks her temperature, which is 37.6 degC. There is no localised wedge erythema, no discrete hot spot, no fluctuance and no nipple cracks or breakdown. She has no rigors and no myalgia. The infant has had 5 heavy wet nappies and 3 transitional stools in the past 24 hours and is feeding 10-12 times a day with audible swallowing once latched. She is otherwise well, on no medications and has no allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 degC, HR 84 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg.
data_table.Examination: Bilateral diffuse symmetrical breast fullness, shiny tight skin, flattened nipples from oedema. No wedge erythema, no fluctuance.
data_table.Infant: 5 heavy wet nappies, 3 transitional stools, 10-12 feeds/24h, audible swallowing.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Commence oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 7 days starting now
options.rationale: Antibiotics are not indicated for bilateral symmetric engorgement without localised erythema or systemic features (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Frequent feeding, reverse-pressure softening, cold packs, simple analgesia and review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Engorgement on day 3-5 is physiological and managed by ensuring effective milk removal: feed often, soften the areola with reverse-pressure softening or hand expression to allow latch, and use cold between feeds for analgesia; antibiotics are not indicated unless localised mastitis develops (ABA; ABM).

options.text: Pump both breasts to empty every 2 hours and supplement infant with formula until oedema settles
options.rationale: Aggressive pump-to-empty strategies overstimulate supply and worsen engorgement; supplementation is not required while transfer is adequate (ABA).

options.text: Apply continuous hot packs for 24 hours combined with deep tissue breast massage
options.rationale: Updated guidance specifically discourages heat and deep massage in the engorgement-mastitis spectrum because they increase oedema and inflammation (ABM 2022).

options.text: Bind both breasts tightly with crepe bandage and stop feeding for 24 hours
options.rationale: Binding and feed cessation cause stasis, worsen pain and risk progression to mastitis or supply suppression (ABA).

explanation.summary: Physiological engorgement peaks at day 3-5 and is bilateral, symmetric and without systemic features. Management is conservative: frequent feeding, reverse-pressure softening to allow latch over an oedematous areola, cold packs and simple analgesia. Antibiotics are reserved for true bacterial mastitis.
key_points: Engorgement: bilateral, symmetric, no localised erythema
Reverse-pressure softening creates space for the infant to latch
Cold preferred to heat per ABM 2022
Avoid binding (causes stasis) and avoid over-pumping (worsens supply)
Low-grade temperature alone does not equal mastitis
explanation.pearls: A flattened nipple from areolar oedema can be the only mechanical reason a previously well-feeding baby suddenly struggles to latch on day 4.

subtopic_detail: Engorgement vs mastitis discrimination

stem: A 30-year-old primiparous woman attends the GP at 3 weeks postpartum with her term infant. She has persistent bilateral cracked, bleeding nipples that have not improved despite three lactation consultant reviews focused on positioning and attachment; the latch has been optimised across multiple holds and she has tried nipple shields without lasting benefit. Feeds last 50-70 minutes and the infant frequently breaks suction with a clicking sound. Infant weight gain has fallen below the expected trajectory: birthweight 3.5 kg, discharge weight 3.25 kg, today's weight 3.4 kg at 3 weeks (mean gain ~10 g/day from nadir, below the 20-30 g/day threshold). On examination by an experienced lactation consultant, the infant has a thin anterior tongue frenulum that restricts tongue elevation to less than midline when crying, a heart-shaped tongue tip when the tongue is lifted, and a Hazelbaker tongue-function score that meets criteria for ankyloglossia with impaired function. The infant has no other oral, craniofacial or neurological abnormalities and is otherwise well.

data_table.Infant assessment: Birthweight 3.5 kg, nadir 3.25 kg, today 3.4 kg at day 21 (~10 g/day from nadir, below threshold).
data_table.Feeding: 50-70 min feeds, audible clicking, nipple trauma persists.
data_table.Examination: Restricted tongue elevation, heart-shaped tongue tip, Hazelbaker confirms functionally significant anterior tongue tie.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer to an experienced clinician for assessment and frenotomy of the anterior tongue tie
options.rationale: When ankyloglossia is associated with functionally significant feeding problems persisting despite optimised positioning and attachment (nipple trauma + impaired transfer + objective tongue-function deficit), frenotomy by an experienced clinician is appropriate and improves both maternal pain and infant transfer in RCT-level evidence (RANZCOG; RCH; ABM Protocol 11).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Tongue tie

options.text: Continue positioning and attachment optimisation for another 4 weeks before considering frenotomy
options.rationale: Positioning has already been optimised with three lactation consultant reviews and infant growth is faltering; further delay risks supply failure and breastfeeding cessation (RANZCOG; ABM).

options.text: Commence formula top-ups and discontinue breastfeeding given the persistent nipple trauma
options.rationale: Mother wishes to continue and a reversible structural cause has been identified; cessation is not first-line when a treatable lesion is present (RCH; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Tongue tie

options.text: Refer for posterior tongue tie and upper-lip tie release out of hours regardless of findings
options.rationale: RANZCOG and RCH caution against indiscriminate posterior tongue tie or upper-lip tie release; the evidence base is weak and over-diagnosis is well documented (RANZCOG; RCH).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Tongue tie

options.text: Prescribe long-term nipple shields and review at the 6-week postnatal check
options.rationale: Shields have already failed in this case and do not address the underlying restriction; reliance on shields can worsen transfer (RCH).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Tongue tie

explanation.summary: Frenotomy for ankyloglossia is indicated only when feeding is functionally impaired AND optimised positioning/attachment has not resolved the issue AND examination confirms restricted tongue function. RANZCOG and RCH caution against routine posterior tongue or lip tie release.
key_points: Frenotomy indicated only when function is impaired
Optimise positioning and attachment with IBCLC support first
RCT evidence supports anterior tongue-tie frenotomy when functionally significant
Posterior tongue tie and lip tie release evidence base is weak
Refer to an experienced clinician (paediatrician, ENT, paediatric dentist with training)
explanation.pearls: If the latch has been optimised across multiple reviews and the mother still has nipple trauma, look in the baby's mouth - not at the mother's technique.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Tongue tie

subtopic_detail: Tongue tie - indications for frenotomy referral

stem: A 34-year-old G1P1 woman attends her GP at 8 weeks postpartum with a 4-week history of low mood, anhedonia, marked early-morning waking despite the infant sleeping through, ruminative guilt about her mothering and a recently-completed EPDS score of 17 with positive endorsement of item 10 (passive death ideation, no plan, no intent, no access). Risk assessment with the GP confirms no active suicidal plan, no psychotic features and no infanticidal thoughts. She has previously had an episode of moderate depression at age 24 that responded well to sertraline, which she continued for 12 months without significant adverse effects, then ceased without recurrence. She is breastfeeding her healthy term infant exclusively, the infant is thriving on the 50th centile, and she wishes to continue breastfeeding. She has no other medications, no medical comorbidities, no QT-prolonging drugs and a normal ECG taken antenatally.

data_table.Mood: EPDS 17. Item 10 positive (passive ideation, no plan).
data_table.History: Prior MDE responsive to sertraline, no other psychiatric history.
data_table.Infant: Exclusively breastfed, thriving (50th centile), term, healthy.

lead_in: Which antidepressant is the most appropriate first-line choice?

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily, titrating to clinical response over 2-4 weeks
options.rationale: Sertraline has the strongest breastfeeding safety data with very low milk:plasma ratio and infant relative-dose typically below 2%, and she has a prior excellent response to it. RANZCP and LactMed list it as a preferred SSRI in breastfeeding (RANZCP Perinatal MH; LactMed).

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily as a first-line breastfeeding-compatible SSRI
options.rationale: Fluoxetine and its long-half-life active metabolite norfluoxetine accumulate in the breastfed infant and have caused colic, irritability and poor feeding in case reports; it is not preferred when starting de novo (LactMed; RANZCP).

options.text: Citalopram 20 mg orally daily as the initial choice while she is breastfeeding
options.rationale: Citalopram has higher milk transfer and more adverse infant reports than sertraline and is associated with dose-dependent QT prolongation; it is an acceptable second-line but not first-line when starting fresh (LactMed; AMH).

options.text: Mirtazapine 30 mg orally at night for sleep and appetite while breastfeeding
options.rationale: Mirtazapine is reasonable when sedation/appetite are dominant features but data in lactation are more limited than sertraline; not first-line for a typical perinatal MDE presentation (RANZCP).

options.text: Defer all pharmacotherapy and await perinatal CBT in approximately 4 weeks
options.rationale: Moderate-to-severe symptoms with EPDS 17 and passive ideation warrant combined psychological plus pharmacological therapy; deferring medication risks deterioration during the wait (RANZCP; beyondblue Perinatal MH guidelines).

explanation.summary: Sertraline is the preferred first-line SSRI in breastfeeding women in Australian and international perinatal mental health guidance because of its very low infant relative dose. Past good response to a specific SSRI is a strong reason to re-use that agent.
key_points: Sertraline: preferred first-line in breastfeeding (low milk transfer)
Fluoxetine: long-half-life metabolite accumulates in infant
Citalopram/escitalopram: acceptable but more reports of adverse infant effects, dose-dependent QT
Past responder to a specific SSRI - re-use it
Moderate-severe perinatal depression warrants combined therapy
explanation.pearls: EPDS item 10 positivity requires same-visit suicide risk assessment regardless of total score.

subtopic_detail: SSRI choice in breastfeeding mother with new perinatal depression

stem: A 32-year-old G2P2 woman, 5 days post-vaginal birth of a healthy term infant, presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of pleuritic right-sided chest pain, mild dyspnoea on exertion and a swollen tender right calf. Bedside observations and ECG are detailed below. CTPA confirms a segmental right lower lobe pulmonary embolus and Doppler ultrasound shows acute right popliteal-femoral DVT. She has no haemodynamic instability, no right heart strain and no hypoxia at rest. She is exclusively breastfeeding her term, well infant and is highly motivated to continue. She has no other medical conditions, takes no medications, has no allergies, no history of bleeding disorder and her postnatal Hb is 118 g/L with normal platelets, INR and APTT.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102 bpm, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 96% room air, T 37.0 degC.
data_table.Investigations: CTPA: segmental RLL PE. Doppler: right popliteal-femoral DVT. Hb 118 g/L, platelets 240 x10^9/L, INR 1.0, APTT 32 s, eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate anticoagulant regimen?

options.text: Therapeutic enoxaparin transitioning to warfarin with bridging until INR therapeutic; continue breastfeeding
options.rationale: Both LMWH (negligible oral bioavailability in the infant) and warfarin (high protein binding, undetectable in milk) are compatible with breastfeeding and are the RANZCOG-recommended options for postpartum VTE; DOACs are NOT recommended in lactation due to insufficient safety data (RANZCOG VTE; LactMed).
source_refs: RANZCOG: VTE in pregnancy and puerperium

options.text: Apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily, continuing breastfeeding
options.rationale: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran) are not recommended in lactation; manufacturer data and LactMed advise against use because safety in the breastfed infant is not established (LactMed; AMH).

options.text: Rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 21 days then 20 mg daily, continuing breastfeeding
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban is excreted into breast milk in animal studies and is not recommended in lactation; LMWH or warfarin are preferred (LactMed; AMH).

options.text: Unfractionated heparin infusion only with discontinuation of breastfeeding for the treatment period
options.rationale: UFH is also compatible with breastfeeding but is not preferred over LMWH for routine VTE outside of renal failure or imminent procedure; cessation of breastfeeding is unnecessary (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: VTE in pregnancy and puerperium

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily and graduated compression stockings as primary treatment for VTE
options.rationale: Aspirin is not adequate treatment for an acute PE or DVT; therapeutic anticoagulation is required (RANZCOG; eTG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: VTE in pregnancy and puerperium

explanation.summary: Postpartum VTE in a breastfeeding woman is treated with LMWH (preferred for ease and predictability) or warfarin; both are compatible with breastfeeding. DOACs are NOT recommended in lactation due to inadequate infant safety data.
key_points: LMWH: negligible oral bioavailability in infant - safe in lactation
Warfarin: high protein binding, undetectable in milk - safe in lactation
DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran): avoid in lactation
Acute PE/DVT requires therapeutic, not prophylactic, anticoagulation
Duration usually at least 3 months for provoked postpartum VTE
explanation.pearls: Tell the patient explicitly that LMWH and warfarin do not require pump-and-dump - common misconception that interrupts breastfeeding unnecessarily.

sources.label: RANZCOG: VTE in pregnancy and puerperium

subtopic_detail: Anticoagulation choice in breastfeeding mother

stem: A 33-year-old G2P2 woman is reviewed in the GP-IBCLC combined clinic at 5 weeks postpartum with confirmed insufficient milk supply contributing to faltering infant growth despite four weeks of structured input. Positioning and attachment have been optimised, frequency of feeds increased, post-feed expression added, and a structural cause has been excluded (no tongue tie, no breast surgery, no retained placenta, normal thyroid). Infant weight gain remains below 20 g/day with documented inadequate transfer on test-weighs over 24 hours. The mother wishes to use a galactagogue as adjunctive therapy to maintain breastfeeding while continuing the non-pharmacological measures. She has no personal cardiac history, but on review of her medications she takes citalopram 20 mg daily for anxiety. Her resting baseline ECG today shows sinus rhythm at 78 bpm with QTc 460 ms (Bazett) and normal electrolytes including K+ 4.4 mmol/L and Mg2+ 0.85 mmol/L. She has no family history of sudden cardiac death and no syncope. The IBCLC asks specifically whether domperidone is appropriate in her situation.

data_table.ECG: Sinus rhythm 78 bpm, QTc 460 ms (Bazett), no AV block.
data_table.Electrolytes: K+ 4.4 mmol/L, Mg2+ 0.85 mmol/L, Ca2+ corrected 2.35 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding domperidone?

options.text: Commence domperidone 10 mg three times daily for 7 days as a galactagogue
options.rationale: TGA and ABM safety guidance contraindicate domperidone where there is concurrent QT-prolonging medication and a QTc at or above 450 ms; citalopram is on the QT-prolonging list and her QTc is 460 ms (TGA; ABM Protocol 9).

options.text: Avoid domperidone; review citalopram with her psychiatrist and intensify non-pharmacological measures
options.rationale: Domperidone is contraindicated when QTc is prolonged or when a patient is on concurrent QT-prolonging drugs (including citalopram); the safe step is to avoid it, address the modifiable risk (review citalopram) and continue evidence-based milk-removal optimisation (TGA; ABM).

options.text: Increase domperidone dose to 20 mg four times daily to maximise the milk yield
options.rationale: TGA limits dosing to no more than 10 mg three times daily because cardiac risk is dose-dependent; in addition she has active contraindications (TGA).

options.text: Substitute metoclopramide 10 mg three times daily as a long-term galactagogue
options.rationale: Metoclopramide has weaker galactagogue evidence and significant risks of extrapyramidal effects and depression with prolonged use; not appropriate as a long-term substitute (ABM).

options.text: Commence fenugreek 600 mg three times daily as a herbal galactagogue without other changes
options.rationale: Fenugreek has limited and inconsistent evidence and is not a substitute for addressing milk removal or for safety review of QT-prolonging medications (ABM).

explanation.summary: Domperidone is the most-evidenced pharmacological galactagogue but carries dose-dependent QT prolongation risk. TGA advice is to avoid use with concurrent QT-prolonging drugs (including citalopram, escitalopram, ondansetron, clarithromycin, methadone) or with a baseline QTc >=450 ms.
key_points: TGA dose ceiling: 10 mg TDS
Baseline ECG indicated before starting domperidone
Avoid with concurrent QT-prolonging drugs
Avoid with QTc >=450 ms or electrolyte derangement
Galactagogues are adjuncts, not replacements, for milk removal
explanation.pearls: Always review the medication list (including SSRIs and antiemetics) before starting domperidone - the most common safety oversight is concurrent QT-prolonging drugs.

subtopic_detail: Domperidone as galactagogue - safety considerations

stem: A 71-year-old G4P4 postmenopausal woman attends a urogynaecology outpatient clinic with 18 months of progressive vaginal pressure that is now associated with a visible bulge at the introitus when she stands at the end of the day, occasional difficulty completing defaecation that improves when she splints the posterior vaginal wall, and intermittent urinary hesitancy. Speculum and POP-Q examination on maximal Valsalva in the supine position record the following measurements: Aa -1, Ba +1, C +3, GH 5 cm, PB 3 cm, TVL 9 cm, Ap -2, Bp -2, D +1. The most dependent point of prolapse is therefore the cervix at +3 cm beyond the hymen.

data_table.POP-Q (supine, Valsalva): Aa -1, Ba +1, C +3, GH 5, PB 3, TVL 9, Ap -2, Bp -2, D +1.
data_table.Examination: Cervix descends 3 cm beyond hymen; anterior and posterior walls supported at hymen or above; PVR 30 mL.

lead_in: Which is the most accurate description of this prolapse?

options.text: POP-Q stage 2 anterior compartment prolapse (cystocele)
options.rationale: The most dependent point is C (cervix) at +3, not Ba; the anterior wall is only at Ba +1, which is stage 2 in isolation, but the dominant defect is apical (RANZCOG; IUGA).

options.text: POP-Q stage 3 apical (uterine) prolapse
options.rationale: POP-Q staging uses the most dependent point: C at +3 (>1 cm beyond hymen but TVL is 9 cm so not complete eversion) is stage 3, with the apex (uterus) being the dominant compartment. Stage 4 would require complete eversion (most distal point at +/- TVL - 2 cm) (IUGA/ICS).

options.text: POP-Q stage 4 complete vaginal vault eversion
options.rationale: Stage 4 requires the most distal point at TVL minus 2 cm or beyond (essentially complete eversion); cervix at +3 with TVL 9 cm does not meet this criterion, and vault prolapse implies prior hysterectomy (IUGA).

options.text: POP-Q stage 3 posterior compartment prolapse (rectocele)
options.rationale: Bp is -2, well above the hymen; the posterior compartment is not the dominant or staged defect (IUGA).

options.text: POP-Q stage 1 mixed compartment prolapse
options.rationale: Stage 1 requires the most distal point >1 cm above the hymen; cervix at +3 is well beyond that threshold (IUGA).

explanation.summary: POP-Q stage is determined by the most distal point: stage 0 no prolapse; stage 1 >1 cm above hymen; stage 2 within 1 cm of hymen; stage 3 >1 cm beyond hymen but not full eversion; stage 4 essentially complete eversion (within 2 cm of TVL). The most dependent point identifies the dominant compartment.
key_points: Six points: Aa, Ba (anterior); Ap, Bp (posterior); C (cervix/cuff), D (posterior fornix if uterus present)
TVL, GH and PB are static landmarks
Most distal point sets the stage
Stage 4 requires near-complete eversion (TVL - 2 cm)
Vault prolapse = apical descent in a post-hysterectomy patient
explanation.pearls: Always identify the dominant compartment before counselling on management; apical defects often co-exist with anterior wall descent and address them together.

subtopic_detail: POP-Q staging and compartment identification

stem: A 64-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman attends urogynaecology with 12 months of progressive vaginal vault prolapse 9 years after a total abdominal hysterectomy for benign menorrhagia. She has previously trialled a ring pessary for 6 months with recurrent expulsion despite refit, and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy for 4 months with no symptomatic benefit. POP-Q examination shows vaginal vault descent to +4 with anterior wall to +2 and posterior wall to 0; TVL is 8 cm. She is sexually active and wishes to preserve coital function; she has no obesity-related surgical risk (BMI 24), no significant cardiopulmonary disease, no diabetes and is otherwise healthy with no contraindication to general anaesthetic and abdominal surgery.

data_table.History: TAH 9 years ago. Pessary failed (recurrent expulsion). PFMT 4 months no benefit. Sexually active, BMI 24, no comorbidities.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate surgical procedure?

options.text: Laparoscopic sacrocolpopexy with type 1 polypropylene mesh fixed to the anterior sacral ligament
options.rationale: Sacrocolpopexy (open or laparoscopic) using type 1 macroporous polypropylene mesh is the gold-standard procedure for symptomatic vault prolapse where coital function is to be preserved; it has the lowest recurrence rate and best preserves vaginal length; abdominal (non-vaginal) mesh placement is not subject to the TGA's transvaginal mesh restriction (RANZCOG; NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Transvaginal mesh kit repair of the vaginal vault using a synthetic mesh device
options.rationale: Transvaginal mesh kits for prolapse repair were withdrawn by the TGA in Australia in 2017 due to harm signals and are not available (TGA mesh statement).

options.text: Sacrospinous ligament fixation of the vaginal vault through a vaginal surgical approach
options.rationale: Sacrospinous fixation is a reasonable native-tissue alternative, particularly for patients who are poor candidates for abdominal surgery, but has higher recurrence and tends to shorten and posteriorly deviate the vagina; sacrocolpopexy is more durable and preserves vaginal axis in fit candidates (RANZCOG; NICE).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Le Fort colpocleisis with obliteration of the vagina for definitive vault support
options.rationale: Colpocleisis is an obliterative procedure reserved for older, frail, non-sexually-active women who accept loss of coital function; she remains sexually active (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat pessary trial with a Gellhorn pessary plus indefinite topical oestrogen therapy
options.rationale: She has already failed conservative management with recurrent pessary expulsion and ineffective PFMT and now elects surgery; another pessary is unlikely to succeed for a stage 3 vault defect with this profile (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Sacrocolpopexy (open or laparoscopic) with type 1 polypropylene mesh is the durability gold-standard for symptomatic vault prolapse where sexual function is to be preserved. Sacrospinous fixation is the principal native-tissue alternative. Colpocleisis is reserved for frail, non-sexually-active patients. Transvaginal mesh kits are not available in Australia since 2017.
key_points: TGA withdrew transvaginal mesh kits in 2017; sacrocolpopexy mesh is placed abdominally and remains available
Sacrocolpopexy preserves vaginal length and axis better than vaginal approaches
Sacrospinous fixation: native tissue, vaginal route, higher recurrence
Colpocleisis: obliterative, only for non-sexually-active
Conservative measures should be trialled first unless not feasible
explanation.pearls: The Australian Senate Inquiry into transvaginal mesh shaped the current TGA stance and is why the abdominal vs vaginal mesh distinction is essential to know.

sources.label: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

subtopic_detail: Vault prolapse after hysterectomy - surgical choice

stem: A 58-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman presents to her GP with 6 months of a vaginal bulge that worsens by the end of the day and after prolonged standing. The bulge has progressed to a sensation of incomplete defaecation, and she has noted she now consistently has to insert two fingers into the posterior vagina and push backwards to allow her to fully evacuate stool, particularly after attempting to pass a formed motion. She has no urinary symptoms (no stress leakage, no urgency, no incomplete voiding sensation, no recurrent UTIs), no faecal incontinence and no dyspareunia. Bowel motions are otherwise normal in frequency and Bristol type 4 consistency with the help of a daily fibre supplement. She is otherwise well, BMI 26, postmenopausal for 8 years, on no medications. On examination, speculum and POP-Q reveal Bp +1 with the posterior wall bulging into the introitus on Valsalva; Ba is -2 and C is -5. Post-void residual is 25 mL.

data_table.Symptoms: Vaginal bulge, splints posterior wall to defaecate, no urinary symptoms.
data_table.POP-Q: Ba -2, Bp +1, C -5. Posterior wall bulges to introitus on Valsalva.

lead_in: Which compartment defect best explains her presentation?

options.text: Anterior vaginal compartment defect, a cystocele
options.rationale: Cystoceles typically present with urinary symptoms (hesitancy, incomplete emptying, splinting the anterior wall to void) and Ba beyond the hymen; her Ba is -2 and she has no urinary symptoms (IUGA).

options.text: Posterior vaginal compartment defect, a rectocele
options.rationale: Defaecatory dysfunction with posterior vaginal wall splinting and Bp at +1 with bulging through the introitus is the classic presentation of a rectocele (RANZCOG; IUGA).

options.text: Apical compartment defect of the uterine cervix
options.rationale: C is -5, well above the hymen, so the cervix has not descended; symptoms are localised to posterior wall splinting, not generalised vaginal heaviness from apical descent (IUGA).

options.text: Enterocele of the pouch of Douglas
options.rationale: Enterocele typically produces upper posterior wall bulging with small bowel contents palpable on bimanual examination and is most often seen post-hysterectomy; while it can co-exist, the dominant clinical picture here is a true rectocele (IUGA; RANZCOG).

options.text: Post-hysterectomy vaginal vault prolapse defect
options.rationale: Vault prolapse describes apical descent in a post-hysterectomy patient; she has not had a hysterectomy and her cervix is in normal position (IUGA).

explanation.summary: Compartment defects map to distinctive symptoms: anterior (cystocele) - urinary hesitancy/splinting anterior wall to void; posterior (rectocele) - obstructed defaecation/splinting posterior wall to evacuate; apical - generalised vaginal heaviness/dragging.
key_points: Anterior wall splinting to void -> cystocele
Posterior wall splinting to defaecate -> rectocele
Generalised heaviness/something coming down -> apical defect
Vault prolapse = apical descent post-hysterectomy
Multiple compartments often coexist - identify the dominant symptom
explanation.pearls: Splinting direction is one of the most useful pieces of history in distinguishing compartment dominance before you examine.

subtopic_detail: Rectocele vs cystocele - symptom discrimination

stem: A 78-year-old woman with mild cognitive impairment (MoCA 24) presents to her GP with 9 months of urinary urgency, urge incontinence approximately twice daily and nocturia 3 times. She has had a structured trial of bladder training and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy for 12 weeks under a continence nurse, with caffeine and evening fluid restriction implemented, and reports modest but inadequate improvement. There is no stress component on her bladder diary, no haematuria, and PVR is 30 mL. Urinalysis is clear; recent FBC, UEC, glucose and Ca2+ are normal. She has well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg, no untreated narrow-angle glaucoma, no severe constipation and her resting heart rate is 72 bpm with BP 134/82 mmHg. She does not have severe uncontrolled hypertension. She is concerned about adding medications that might worsen her memory. Her geriatrician and GP agree to add pharmacotherapy to her ongoing conservative measures.

data_table.Bladder diary: Urgency, urge UI x2/day, nocturia x3, no stress component. PVR 30 mL.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate pharmacological agent?

options.text: Oxybutynin immediate-release 5 mg orally twice a day
options.rationale: Oxybutynin (especially IR) is the antimuscarinic with the highest CNS penetration and is associated with cognitive decline in older adults; it is listed as potentially inappropriate in older people with cognitive impairment in the Beers and AMH guidance (AMH; Beers Criteria).

options.text: Mirabegron 25 mg orally daily, titrating to 50 mg if needed
options.rationale: Mirabegron is a beta-3 adrenergic agonist with no anticholinergic burden, making it preferred in older patients with cognitive impairment when conservative measures are inadequate, provided severe uncontrolled hypertension is excluded (NICE NG123; Continence Foundation Australia).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Solifenacin 5 mg orally daily as a long-acting antimuscarinic
options.rationale: Solifenacin is a more bladder-selective antimuscarinic with lower CNS penetration than oxybutynin but still carries anticholinergic burden best avoided in cognitive impairment; reasonable second-line in those without dementia features (AMH).

options.text: Duloxetine 40 mg orally twice daily for the urinary symptoms
options.rationale: Duloxetine is used for stress urinary incontinence; her diary shows no stress component (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Desmopressin 0.1 mg orally at night for the urge incontinence
options.rationale: Desmopressin is restricted in older patients due to high risk of hyponatraemia and is not first-line for OAB; the Continence Foundation and AMH advise against use in those over 65 outside very specific indications (Continence Foundation Australia; AMH).

explanation.summary: Second-line pharmacotherapy for urge UI in an older person with cognitive impairment should minimise anticholinergic burden. Mirabegron is preferred provided uncontrolled severe hypertension is excluded. Antimuscarinics carry cognitive and falls risk; oxybutynin IR is the worst offender.
key_points: First-line: bladder training, PFMT, caffeine reduction, weight loss
Mirabegron: beta-3 agonist, no anticholinergic burden, check BP
Antimuscarinics rank by CNS burden: oxybutynin (worst) > tolterodine > solifenacin/darifenacin (lowest)
Desmopressin restricted in >65 (hyponatraemia)
Duloxetine is for stress UI, not urge UI
explanation.pearls: Always check PVR before pharmacotherapy in OAB - antimuscarinics in undiagnosed chronic retention can precipitate acute retention.

sources.label: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

subtopic_detail: Urge incontinence pharmacological choice in older woman

stem: A 52-year-old G3P3 woman attends with 12 months of urinary leakage. On a 3-day bladder diary she records: 8 leakage episodes total - 6 preceded by sudden urgency (mostly on the way to the toilet or hearing running water), and 2 small-volume leaks on coughing or sneezing. Daytime void frequency is 9, nocturia 2, urgency score on Patient Perception of Bladder Condition is severe, and quality-of-life impact is dominated by the urge episodes (she has stopped attending her grandson's school events because of fear of leakage with running to a toilet). PVR is 40 mL, urinalysis is negative, and a cough stress test reproduces a small leak with full bladder. BMI 31, no smoking, no relevant medications, no neurological symptoms. She declines surgery at this stage and has not yet had any structured conservative therapy or pharmacotherapy.

data_table.Bladder diary: 8 leakage episodes; 6 urge, 2 stress. Frequency 9, nocturia 2.
data_table.Tests: PVR 40 mL, urinalysis negative, cough stress test +ve (small leak).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management approach?

options.text: PFMT, bladder training, weight loss and caffeine reduction; review at 3 months
options.rationale: NICE and Continence Foundation Australia advise structured conservative therapy as initial management for mixed UI, with treatment then directed at the predominant symptom (urge here). PFMT helps both components, bladder training targets urge, and weight loss helps both; structured trial for at least 3 months before escalation (NICE NG123; Continence Foundation Australia).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Refer immediately for a tension-free vaginal tape mid-urethral sling procedure
options.rationale: Surgery is not first-line for mixed UI, and stress-incontinence surgery may worsen pre-existing urge symptoms; she also declines surgery (RANZCOG; NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Commence an antimuscarinic plus duloxetine combination from the outset of management
options.rationale: Combined pharmacotherapy from outset is not recommended; conservative therapy is first-line, with single-agent pharmacotherapy directed at the predominant symptom if conservative therapy is inadequate (NICE).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Refer for invasive urodynamic studies before offering any conservative management
options.rationale: Urodynamics are not required before conservative management of uncomplicated mixed UI; they are reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, prior incontinence surgery, suspected voiding dysfunction or where surgery is being planned (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Insert a continence pessary and commence indefinite topical oestrogen therapy
options.rationale: Continence pessaries are useful adjuncts mainly for stress UI driven by hypermobility, and topical oestrogen is reserved for urogenital atrophy; neither addresses her predominant urge component (Continence Foundation Australia).

explanation.summary: Mixed UI is managed with a structured conservative bundle first (PFMT at least 3 months, bladder training, weight loss if BMI >30, caffeine and fluid optimisation, smoking cessation). When pharmacotherapy is needed, target the predominant symptom; urodynamics are reserved for selected indications.
key_points: Bladder diary identifies the predominant component
PFMT >=3 months helps both stress and urge
Weight loss of 5-10% halves leakage episodes
Pharmacotherapy targets predominant symptom (antimuscarinic/mirabegron for urge; duloxetine for stress)
Urodynamics not routine before conservative management
explanation.pearls: Treating the predominant component first often unmasks or relieves the secondary component without separate intervention.

sources.label: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

subtopic_detail: Mixed urinary incontinence - prioritising treatment by predominant symptom

stem: A 72-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.4%, on metformin + dapagliflozin) presents with 6 months of small-volume continuous urinary leakage, daytime urinary frequency 12, nocturia 4 with leakage on rising, and a sensation of incomplete emptying. She has no urgency that precedes the leakage. Her GP has been treating her empirically as urge incontinence with oxybutynin for 8 weeks with worsening of her leakage. On examination she has a palpable bladder to the umbilicus, mildly impaired ankle reflexes with reduced light touch and pinprick over both feet bilaterally, and no costovertebral angle tenderness. Speculum reveals a stage 2 cystocele to the hymen with no other prolapse. Urinalysis: protein 1+, no nitrites, no leucocytes. Bedside bladder scan post-void shows a PVR of 720 mL. Bloods: Hb 118 g/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 88), eGFR 36 mL/min/1.73m2 (down from 68), K+ 5.3 mmol/L, HbA1c 8.4%. Renal tract ultrasound shows bilateral hydronephrosis.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/84 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Examination: Palpable bladder to umbilicus, peripheral neuropathy, stage 2 cystocele.
data_table.Investigations: PVR 720 mL on bladder scan. Creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 88), eGFR 36, K+ 5.3 mmol/L. Renal USS: bilateral hydronephrosis. Urinalysis: protein 1+, no infection.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Cease oxybutynin, insert indwelling catheter and refer urgently to urology
options.rationale: PVR 720 mL with new bilateral hydronephrosis and rising creatinine indicates high-pressure chronic retention with obstructive AKI - bladder decompression with an indwelling catheter and urgent urology review is the immediate priority; antimuscarinics worsen retention and must be stopped (NICE NG112; Continence Foundation Australia; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: NICE NG112 Lower urinary tract symptoms in men (decompression principles applicable)

options.text: Add mirabegron 50 mg daily to the oxybutynin and review again in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Adding further pharmacotherapy for assumed OAB ignores high-volume retention with renal compromise; doing so risks worsening retention and renal injury (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Refer for urgent mid-urethral sling surgery for presumed stress urinary incontinence now
options.rationale: She has overflow incontinence from chronic retention, not stress incontinence; a sling in this context will precipitate complete retention and worsen renal injury (NICE).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Commence frusemide 40 mg daily to manage her presumed nocturnal polyuria
options.rationale: Frusemide increases urine production into an already overloaded obstructed bladder and worsens both retention and AKI (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Arrange outpatient urodynamics in 6 weeks and continue current therapy unchanged
options.rationale: Outpatient delay is unsafe when there is post-obstructive AKI with bilateral hydronephrosis; immediate bladder decompression is required (NICE; Continence Foundation Australia).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

explanation.summary: Continuous small-volume leakage with high PVR, palpable bladder, and rising creatinine with hydronephrosis is overflow incontinence from high-pressure chronic retention with post-obstructive AKI. Immediate bladder decompression with an indwelling catheter and urgent urology review is mandatory. Monitor for post-obstructive diuresis and hyperkalaemia.
key_points: Overflow UI: continuous leakage, high PVR, no urgency, often diabetic or neurogenic
High-pressure retention with hydronephrosis + rising creatinine = obstructive AKI
Decompress immediately with IDC
Stop all antimuscarinics
Watch for post-obstructive diuresis (replace half losses cautiously)
explanation.pearls: Any patient on antimuscarinics whose leakage gets worse should have a PVR scan before any further dose change - the most common missed cause of overflow incontinence is iatrogenic.

sources.label: NICE NG112 Lower urinary tract symptoms in men (decompression principles applicable)
NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

subtopic_detail: Overflow incontinence with chronic urinary retention

stem: A 55-year-old G3P3 woman is referred to urogynaecology after 12 months of stress urinary incontinence symptoms refractory to a documented 6-month programme of supervised pelvic floor muscle training and weight loss (lost 7 kg, BMI now 28). Her bladder diary shows leakage with cough/sneeze/exertion only, no urgency or urge episodes, no voiding difficulty. PVR is 20 mL and a positive cough stress test reproduces leakage in clinic. There is no haematuria, no recurrent UTI, no prior pelvic surgery, no neurological disease and no prolapse beyond the hymen (POP-Q Ba -1). She has been counselled about a mid-urethral sling and an autologous fascial sling and is keen to proceed surgically. She has not had urodynamic studies. The clinical fellow asks whether urodynamics should be performed before sling surgery. She has not had any previous incontinence surgery, no pelvic radiotherapy and no relevant neurological disease.

data_table.Diary: Pure stress symptoms; no urgency or urge.
data_table.Tests: PVR 20 mL, positive cough stress test, no prolapse beyond hymen.
data_table.Trials: 6 months PFMT + 7 kg weight loss without resolution.

lead_in: Which approach to urodynamics is most consistent with current guidance?

options.text: Routine urodynamic studies in every woman before any incontinence surgery
options.rationale: Current NICE NG123 advises against routine urodynamics before primary mid-urethral sling for clinically clear, uncomplicated stress urinary incontinence (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Urodynamics not required before primary sling here; reserve for mixed or complex
options.rationale: Her presentation is uncomplicated pure stress UI with normal PVR, no voiding symptoms, no prior surgery and no prolapse beyond the hymen; NICE NG123 and the Continence Foundation Australia consensus indicate urodynamics in this scenario do not change management (NICE; Continence Foundation Australia).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Urodynamic studies only after a failed primary mid-urethral sling procedure
options.rationale: Failure of a primary procedure is one indication but not the only one; urodynamics may be appropriate before surgery in selected complicated cases (NICE).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Urodynamic studies required only when the post-void residual exceeds 100 mL
options.rationale: PVR is one input; symptom pattern, prior surgery, neurological history and prolapse all guide the decision, not PVR alone (NICE).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Urodynamics is mandatory whenever any pharmacotherapy is being considered
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy for urge UI does not require urodynamics; conservative measures and an empirical trial are first-line (NICE; Continence Foundation Australia).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

explanation.summary: Urodynamic studies are NOT required before a primary procedure for clinically straightforward stress urinary incontinence. They are indicated when: symptoms are mixed or urge-predominant, voiding dysfunction is suspected (high PVR, hesitancy), there is previous incontinence/pelvic surgery, concurrent prolapse repair is planned (occult SUI assessment), or there is neurogenic disease.
key_points: Pure, uncomplicated SUI -> no urodynamics needed before primary sling
Mixed UI with discordant findings -> urodynamics often helpful
Prior incontinence surgery -> urodynamics before redo
Neurogenic bladder -> urodynamics needed
Suspected occult SUI before prolapse surgery -> urodynamics
explanation.pearls: The single biggest indication people forget is concurrent prolapse repair, where reducing the prolapse can unmask stress incontinence.

sources.label: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

subtopic_detail: Indications for urodynamic studies

stem: A 48-year-old G3P3 woman attends the gynaecology outpatient clinic 4 weeks after a total laparoscopic hysterectomy for symptomatic fibroids. The procedure was complicated by adhesions at the vesicouterine peritoneum requiring sharp dissection from the bladder; the surgeon documented bladder integrity at the end of the case using bladder filling without leak observed. For the past 8 days she has had continuous painless watery leakage into her underwear regardless of position or activity, with no urge, no stress trigger, no incomplete emptying sensation, and unchanged daytime voids of normal volume. There is no fever, no flank pain, no haematuria, no vaginal bleeding. Urinalysis is negative for blood, nitrites and leucocytes. Speculum examination reveals clear fluid pooling in the vaginal vault but no visible defect on direct inspection. The vault is well healed; there is no apparent vault dehiscence. She is on no medications other than recent post-operative paracetamol and is otherwise well.

data_table.History: Continuous painless watery leakage 8 days after TLH complicated by bladder dissection.
data_table.Examination: Clear fluid in vault, no visible defect, vault intact.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis clear, no infection, no haematuria.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: Three-swab dye test: instil dilute methylene blue into the bladder and inspect vaginal swabs
options.rationale: The three-swab (tampon) test with intravesical dye distinguishes vesicovaginal from ureterovaginal and urethrovaginal fistulae and can identify a fistula site too small to see at speculum; it is a simple bedside test and is the standard first-line investigation alongside, or before, formal cystoscopy (RANZCOG; FIGO Fistula manual).
source_refs: FIGO Global Competency-Based Fistula Surgery Training Manual

options.text: Empirical oral antibiotics for 7 days then outpatient review of the leakage pattern
options.rationale: Urinalysis is clear and the clinical picture is not UTI; antibiotics will not address a fistula (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Urodynamic studies as first-line to characterise the pattern of continuous leakage
options.rationale: Urodynamics characterise bladder/sphincter function and are not used to diagnose fistula (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: MRI pelvis with rectal contrast to define the suspected genitourinary tract fistula
options.rationale: MRI may help anatomical mapping for surgical planning of complex fistulae but is not the first step; dye testing plus cystoscopy is the standard initial workup (RANZCOG; FIGO).

options.text: Outpatient pelvic floor physiotherapy with re-review in 3 months without imaging
options.rationale: Continuous painless leakage after pelvic surgery with bladder dissection is fistula until proven otherwise; physiotherapy delays diagnosis (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Continuous painless urinary leakage after pelvic surgery is a vesicovaginal or ureterovaginal fistula until proven otherwise. The three-swab dye test (intravesical methylene blue plus tampons in upper, middle and lower vagina) localises the leak and distinguishes urethral, vesical and ureteric sources; cystoscopy and CT-IVU or CTU then characterise the defect for repair planning.
key_points: Continuous painless leakage post-pelvic surgery -> fistula until proven otherwise
Three-swab dye test: upper swab stained suggests ureterovaginal; mid/upper bladder; lower urethrovaginal
If dye test negative for vesical source, suspect ureterovaginal: oral phenazopyridine or IV indigo carmine plus CT-IVU
Cystoscopy maps the bladder defect for repair planning
Obstetric fistula remains the leading global cause; in high-income settings most fistulae are iatrogenic post-hysterectomy
explanation.pearls: If the upper vaginal tampon stains blue and the middle does not, suspect a ureterovaginal fistula and proceed to ureteric imaging.

sources.label: FIGO Global Competency-Based Fistula Surgery Training Manual
NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

subtopic_detail: Vesicovaginal fistula - diagnostic approach

stem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman attends a vulval clinic with 10 months of vulval and vaginal soreness, burning dyspareunia and recurrent contact-bleeding from the vagina. On examination there is glazed erythema of the labia minora and vestibule with a serpiginous white reticular lacy pattern at the inner labia, painful erosions at the introitus, and on speculum examination there is friable erythematous mucosa of the upper vagina with synechiae causing partial vaginal narrowing and obliteration of the vaginal fornices. There is no labial fusion, no figure-of-eight pallor extending around the anus, and the clitoral hood is intact and not buried. She also has lacy white striae on the buccal mucosa and a desquamative gingivitis confirmed by her dentist 6 months ago. There are no skin lesions on the trunk or limbs. Vulval biopsy is taken from the edge of an erosion and shows a band-like sub-epithelial lymphocytic infiltrate with basal cell vacuolar degeneration and Civatte bodies; there is no homogeneous dermal hyalinisation.

data_table.Examination: Wickham striae inner labia, vestibular erosions, vaginal synechiae with friable mucosa.
data_table.Extragenital: Oral lichen planus (buccal lacy striae, desquamative gingivitis).
data_table.Histology: Band-like lymphocytic infiltrate, basal vacuolar degeneration, Civatte bodies. No hyalinisation.

lead_in: Which diagnosis is most consistent with these findings?

options.text: Erosive vulvovaginal lichen planus with classic mucocutaneous involvement
options.rationale: Wickham striae, painful erosions, vaginal involvement with synechiae and friability, concurrent oral lichen planus and the histology of band-like lymphocytic infiltrate with Civatte bodies all point to erosive lichen planus; vaginal involvement is a key feature that distinguishes LP from LS (BSSVD; RCH-Vulval Disorders guidance via Royal Women's Vic).

options.text: Lichen sclerosus with figure-of-eight pallor and architectural change
options.rationale: LS shows porcelain-white atrophic skin with figure-of-eight perianal involvement, labial resorption and clitoral hood phimosis; vaginal mucosa is NOT involved in LS, and histology shows dermal hyalinisation, which is absent here (BSSVD).

options.text: Vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia (HSIL/uVIN) on the vulval mucosa
options.rationale: HSIL/uVIN shows discrete pigmented or whitish plaques with acetowhitening and HPV-related dysplastic epithelium on histology; the inflammatory band, Civatte bodies and oral involvement here are not VIN features (RANZCOG; BSSVD).

options.text: Plasma cell vulvitis (Zoon vulvitis) of the vulval vestibule
options.rationale: Zoon vulvitis presents with glistening orange-red patches without Wickham striae or oral lesions, and histology shows a dense plasma cell infiltrate, not band-like T-cell infiltrate with Civatte bodies (BSSVD).

options.text: Atrophic vaginitis from genitourinary syndrome of menopause
options.rationale: GSM produces diffuse pallor, loss of rugae and dryness without erosions, Wickham striae or oral involvement; histology is not erosive lymphocytic (RANZCOG: Menopause).

explanation.summary: Vulval lichen planus, particularly the erosive subtype, differs from lichen sclerosus in three key ways: vaginal mucosal involvement (synechiae, friability) which LS does NOT cause; Wickham striae and concurrent oral lichen planus; and histology with band-like lymphocytic infiltrate plus Civatte bodies without dermal hyalinisation.
key_points: LP affects vaginal mucosa; LS does not
Wickham striae (lacy reticular pattern) is pathognomonic for LP
Oral lichen planus is present in many erosive cases - examine the mouth
First-line management: high-potency topical clobetasol; vaginal hydrocortisone foam for vaginal involvement
Lichen planus also carries a small but real risk of vulvar SCC and requires long-term surveillance
explanation.pearls: Always examine the mouth in any woman with persistent vulval erosions - erosive LP is multi-mucosal in most cases.

stem: A 38-year-old woman with HIV (CD4 540, viral load undetectable on ART) is referred to a vulval clinic with 9 months of mild vulval pruritus. Examination shows two well-demarcated, slightly raised, pigmented and acetowhite plaques on the right labium minus measuring 8 mm and 12 mm, with no ulceration, induration or vascular atypia. There is no clitoral hood resorption, no labial fusion and no porcelain-white skin. Vulvoscopy-directed punch biopsy from each lesion shows high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (vulvar HSIL, also called usual-type VIN) with full-thickness dysplasia, koilocytotic change and p16 block-positive immunohistochemistry consistent with HPV-driven disease; there is no invasion, no microinvasion and no features of differentiated VIN (no basal atypia on a background of lichen sclerosus). She has had previous CIN 2 treated by LLETZ 5 years ago and is up to date with HPV-based cervical screening. She is sexually active with one partner, both fully HPV-vaccinated, no immunosuppressive medications other than ART, no other comorbidities and is keen to preserve vulval anatomy if possible.

data_table.Lesions: Two pigmented/acetowhite plaques right labium minus (8 mm and 12 mm), no induration.
data_table.Histology: Vulvar HSIL (uVIN), p16 block-positive (HPV-driven), no invasion.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Topical imiquimod 5% three times weekly for up to 16 weeks
options.rationale: For non-invasive, HPV-driven vulvar HSIL (uVIN) in unifocal or limited multifocal lesions where anatomy preservation matters, topical imiquimod is RANZCOG/BSSVD-recommended first-line, with documented clinical clearance in ~50-80% and a re-biopsy threshold for any persistent lesion to exclude occult invasion (RANZCOG; BSSVD).

options.text: Watchful waiting with annual vulvoscopy and no active treatment
options.rationale: HSIL/uVIN carries approximately 5-10% progression risk to invasive SCC if untreated; active management is required (RANZCOG; BSSVD).

options.text: Radical vulvectomy with bilateral inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy for vulval HSIL
options.rationale: Radical vulvectomy with lymphadenectomy is reserved for invasive vulvar carcinoma, not preinvasive HSIL (RANZCOG).

options.text: Topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment to lesions for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Potent topical steroids are used for inflammatory dermatoses (lichen sclerosus, lichen planus), not HSIL/uVIN; they do not treat preinvasive dysplasia (BSSVD).

options.text: Topical 5-fluorouracil cream applied daily indefinitely as primary therapy
options.rationale: 5-FU has been largely supplanted by imiquimod due to high rates of vulval chemical burn and lower tolerability; indefinite application is not recommended (BSSVD; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Vulvar HSIL (uVIN) is HPV-driven and managed with one of: imiquimod (anatomy-sparing first line for selected lesions), wide local excision, or laser ablation. Differentiated VIN (dVIN), associated with lichen sclerosus and HPV-independent, has higher cancer risk and is managed with surgical excision (not imiquimod). Any non-responding or atypical area requires re-biopsy.
key_points: Vulvar HSIL = uVIN, HPV-driven, p16-positive, multifocal more common, lower per-lesion cancer risk
Differentiated VIN = dVIN, HPV-independent, p16-negative, on background of LS, HIGHER cancer risk, surgical excision
Imiquimod: anatomy-sparing for uVIN; ~50-80% clearance
Wide local excision: definitive histology, suitable for unifocal or suspicious lesions
Long-term surveillance after any treatment because of recurrence and dysplasia elsewhere
explanation.pearls: A non-healing or hyperkeratotic vulval lesion in a woman with long-standing lichen sclerosus should make you think dVIN/SCC, not uVIN - excise, do not topical-treat.

stem: A 28-year-old G0 nulliparous woman presents to ED with a 3-day history of progressively painful right-sided vulval swelling, now interfering with walking and sitting. She has no fever, no rigors, no urinary symptoms, no vaginal discharge and no dyspareunia other than from the swelling itself. She is sexually active with one male partner, her last cervical screen was HPV-negative 2 years ago and she has no STI symptoms. On examination there is a tense, exquisitely tender, fluctuant 4 cm mass in the right posterolateral introitus at the 5 o'clock position of the vaginal fornix consistent with a Bartholin abscess; the overlying skin is intact and not necrotic. She is otherwise well, observations normal, and has had no prior episodes. She would like definitive treatment that minimises recurrence and allows her to return to work in a few days.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.1 degC, HR 84 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg.
data_table.Examination: 4 cm fluctuant tender mass right posterolateral introitus at 5 o'clock, intact non-necrotic skin, no cellulitis.
data_table.History: No prior Bartholin episodes, no STI symptoms.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate procedure?

options.text: Simple incision and drainage with packing and pack removal at 48 hours
options.rationale: Simple I&D without a fistulising procedure has the highest recurrence rate (~10-15%) because the gland duct re-stenoses; not preferred as definitive primary treatment (RCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Incision and Word catheter insertion under local anaesthetic for 4-6 weeks
options.rationale: Word catheter insertion is a low-morbidity office or ED procedure under local anaesthetic that creates an epithelialised fistulous tract and is first-line for primary Bartholin abscess, with recurrence rates comparable to marsupialisation (RCOG GTG; KEMH).

options.text: Marsupialisation under general anaesthesia as first-line for any primary abscess
options.rationale: Marsupialisation has equivalent recurrence to Word catheter but requires GA and operating theatre access; it is generally reserved for recurrent disease or when office-based Word catheter has failed (RCOG GTG).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 7 days without drainage
options.rationale: Antibiotics alone do not resolve an established Bartholin abscess; source control is required. Antibiotics are added if cellulitis, systemic features, immunosuppression or recurrent disease (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Excision of the Bartholin gland as primary management of the abscess
options.rationale: Gland excision carries significant bleeding and dyspareunia morbidity and is reserved for recurrent disease after failed drainage procedures or in older women in whom gland malignancy is suspected (RCOG GTG).

explanation.summary: Primary Bartholin abscess is managed with a fistulising drainage procedure - Word catheter insertion is the office/ED first-line; marsupialisation is the operating theatre equivalent. Simple I&D recurs frequently and gland excision is reserved for recurrent failure. Antibiotics are adjunctive only if cellulitis, systemic features, immunosuppression or pregnancy.
key_points: Word catheter: local anaesthetic, low morbidity, in situ 4-6 weeks
Marsupialisation: equivalent recurrence, requires GA
Simple I&D: highest recurrence
Antibiotics adjunctive only with cellulitis, systemic features or immunosuppression
In women aged 40+ with a Bartholin mass, consider biopsy to exclude carcinoma
explanation.pearls: A new Bartholin mass in a postmenopausal woman is carcinoma until biopsy proves otherwise - very different from the under-40 abscess pathway.
